Platform options: Bandcamp (music+merch, 10% cut), Shopify ($29/mo, most professional), BigCartel (free up to 5 products), Spring (POD, zero upfront). Essential: high-quality photos, size chart, shipping timeline, return policy.

I never know how to price my merch. I either feel like I'm overcharging fans or leaving money on the table. How do I price things correctly? [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise","hasProject":true}]

Pricing is psychology as much as it is math. Here's the framework:

Step 1 — Know your costs: Calculate the TOTAL cost per unit: manufacturing cost + shipping to you + packaging (poly bags, stickers) + payment processing (2.6% for Square/Stripe). Example T-shirt: blank + print = $8.50. Poly bag + sticker: $0.50. Total cost: $9.00 per unit.

Step 2 — Set your margin: Minimum healthy margin: 3x cost (for DTC/online sales). At $9 cost: minimum price = $27, round to $28. For live shows: 4-5x cost is achievable ($36-$45) because of the impulse-buy environment and zero shipping costs. For wholesale (to stores): 2x cost ($18), and the store retails at $30-$36.

Step 3 — Use pricing psychology: (1) Price anchoring: place your most expensive item first on your website and at the merch table. A $60 hoodie makes a $28 T-shirt feel like a deal. (2) Charm pricing: $28 instead of $30. $47 instead of $50. Prices ending in 7, 8, or 9 consistently outperform round numbers. (3) Bundle pricing: 'Shirt + hat: $40 (save $8).' Bundles increase average transaction size. The perceived savings drive the purchase. (4) Tiered pricing: offer 3 price points at minimum (e.g., stickers at $5, shirts at $28, hoodies at $50). This gives every budget an option.

Step 4 — Context-specific pricing: Online store: include shipping in the price or offer free shipping above $50 (encourage larger orders). Live shows: round to easy-to-make-change amounts ($5, $10, $25, $30). Festival merch: price 10-15% higher than online — fans have festival spending mindset. Limited editions: premium pricing (20-30% above standard) for numbered/signed/variant items.

Common pricing mistakes: Pricing too LOW because you feel guilty: your fans want to support you. Fair pricing isn't exploitative — it sustains your art. Not accounting for ALL costs (forgetting shipping, payment processing, returns). Not adjusting for different sales channels (online vs live vs wholesale). Having too many items at the same price point — differentiate with a range.

The price testing method: A/B test prices by changing them between shows or between online campaigns. If sales volume doesn't change when you raise the price by $3-$5, you were underpriced.

I want to start selling merch but I don't know what to make. What should my first product be? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"indie"}}]

Start with two products. Don't overthink this — your first merch run is a test, not a fashion line.

**Product 1: Stickers ($0.15-0.50/unit, sell for $2-5)**
Why first: Lowest risk, lowest cost, highest impulse-buy rate. Order 100-200 stickers with your logo or a simple design. Sell at shows, include free with online orders, hand them out at events. They're marketing that people pay you for.

**Product 2: One T-shirt design ($5-12/unit for bulk, $15-20 for POD, sell for $25-35)**
Why: T-shirts are the bread and butter of artist merch. Start with ONE design — not five. Here's how:
- Design: Your artist name or logo in a clean, wearable design. Think 'would someone wear this even if they didn't know the artist?' If yes, good design. If it looks like a flyer, redesign.
- Colors: Black shirt, white/color print. Black sells more than any other color across every genre. By a lot.
- Sizing: If bulk ordering, start with: M(20%), L(35%), XL(30%), XXL(15%). Skip Small for your first run unless you have specific data.
- Start with POD (Printful/Printify) if you're selling online only. Switch to bulk when you have shows to sell at.

**What NOT to make for your first product:**
- Hoodies (expensive unit cost, higher risk)
- Hats (sizing is tricky, lower margins)
- Vinyl (expensive to press, only worth it if you have rabid fans)
- Custom anything (keychains, phone cases, etc. — too niche to start)

**The test mentality:** Your first merch run teaches you what your audience actually buys. Pay attention to which sizes sell first, which design gets more compliments, whether people buy at shows vs. online. Use that data to inform your second run.

How do I track merch inventory across online and tour sales? [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise"}]

Inventory tracking: Use Shopify's built-in inventory (syncs online + POS). For tour: Square POS syncs with your online store. Track by SKU (design + size + color). Set reorder alerts at 20% remaining stock. Post-tour reconciliation: count remaining, record shrinkage, update online stock levels.

Do labels take a cut of merch sales? I've heard about 360 deals. [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"label-deal","stage":"developing","genre":"hip-hop"}}]

360 deals (also called 'multiple rights deals') are the industry standard at major labels — and yes, they typically include a merch cut.

**360 Deal Merch Terms:**

| Deal Type | Label's Merch Cut | Common Terms |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|
| Traditional record deal | 0% (merch is yours) | Rare in 2026 |
| Modified 360 | 10-20% of NET merch revenue | Most common |
| Full 360 | 25-35% of NET merch revenue | Major label standard |
| Merch-specific deal | 50-80% (they fund production) | Universal Merch, Live Nation Merch |

**What to Negotiate:**
1. **Merch carve-out:** Try to exclude merchandise from 360 provisions entirely.
2. **NET vs GROSS:** Ensure the percentage is on NET (after production costs), not GROSS (total revenue).
3. **Online vs live:** Negotiate different rates — online merch margins are lower due to shipping.
4. **Minimum earnings threshold:** 'Label receives merch percentage only after artist earns $X from merch.'
5. **Design approval:** Never give up control of what products bear your name.

**The Independent Advantage:**
As an independent artist, you keep 100% of merch revenue. At a $5-25 margin per item, this is often MORE than streaming income. Think carefully before giving away this revenue stream.

Route to Legal for contract review of any deal that includes merch provisions.

How do I get fans to spend more per order? [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise"}]

Average Order Value (AOV) maximization:

1. FREE SHIPPING THRESHOLD: Set just above current AOV. If AOV is $28, free shipping at $35.
2. BUNDLE DISCOUNTS: 'Any 2 for $40' (normally $25 each) — increases items per order.
3. IMPULSE ADD-ONS: Small items ($3-5 stickers/buttons) at checkout.
4. POST-PURCHASE UPSELL: 'Add a poster for $8?' after checkout confirmation.
5. SIGNED EDITIONS: Charge $5 premium for signed items. Zero production cost.
6. PRODUCT LADDER: Entry ($5 sticker) → Mid ($25 tee) → Premium ($50 hoodie) → Ultra ($100 signed vinyl bundle). Every fan should find their price point.

I see some artists doing pop-up shops when they release albums. Is that worth it for an indie artist or is it just for big names? [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise","hasProject":true}]

Pop-up shops work at EVERY level — scaled appropriately. Here's how to make one work on an indie budget:

Why pop-ups work: (1) Creates an EVENT around your release (not just another social media post). (2) Physical experience builds deeper fan connection than any digital interaction. (3) Higher conversion rate than online — fans who show up are committed. (4) Content goldmine — photos, videos, stories for weeks of social media.

Scales for different levels: Level 1 — The 'Coffee Shop Pop-Up' ($50-$200): Partner with a local coffee shop, record store, or art gallery. Set up a merch table + listening station for 3-4 hours. Play your album through the shop's speakers. Buy 50 coffees for attendees (or negotiate with the shop — they get foot traffic). Cost: basically nothing beyond your merch stock. Great for album releases and local community building.

Level 2 — The 'Space Share' ($200-$1,000): Rent a small retail space, warehouse, or art gallery for one day. Use free classified sites or spacefinder.com. Set up a branded environment: your visual identity on the walls, merch displays, a listening lounge. Include live acoustic performance, meet & greet, signed items. Charge a small entry fee ($5-$10) or free entry with RSVP.

Level 3 — The 'Full Experience' ($1,000-$5,000): Multi-room experience tied to your album concept. Visual art installations, immersive audio rooms, custom vinyl pressing station. Limited edition merch exclusive to the pop-up. Photography booth with branded backdrop. Potential brand sponsor to offset costs.

Maximizing revenue: Pop-up exclusive merch (not available online — creates urgency). Bundle deals ('Vinyl + exclusive pop-up tee + signed poster: $60'). Payment setup: Square reader for cards + cash drawer. Email signup station — capture everyone's email. Pre-sell tickets/RSVPs to gauge attendance and generate advance revenue.

Timing: Align with album release week. Saturday afternoon: 12-6 PM works best. Promote 2-3 weeks ahead via social media and email list. Create an event on Instagram, Eventbrite, or Partiful.

Content capture: Hire a friend or photographer to document EVERYTHING. Instagram Stories throughout the day. TikTok recap video post-event. Email follow-up to attendees within 48 hours ('Thanks for coming — here's a discount code for anything you didn't grab').

Should I do print-on-demand or hold inventory? [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise"}]

POD vs Inventory:

POD: Zero upfront cost, zero risk. Lower margins ($5-8/shirt). Slower shipping. Unlimited SKU testing.

INVENTORY: Higher margins ($12-18/shirt). Same-day shipping. Full quality control. Requires $500-3000 upfront. Inventory risk.

Recommendation by stage:
- Under 1K followers: POD only
- 1K-10K: Hybrid (POD for experiments, inventory for bestsellers)
- 10K+: Inventory for top sellers, POD for limited editions

What's better - screen printing or DTG? [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise"}]

Screen Print: Best for 50+ units same design. $1-3/color setup, lowest per-unit cost at volume. Bold, vibrant, durable. Limited color count per design.

DTG: Best for small runs and complex full-color art. No setup cost. Higher per-unit ($8-15). Softer hand feel. Photo-realistic quality.

Rule: Under 25 units → DTG. 25-50 → either. Over 50 → screen print.

When should I release merch throughout the year? [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise"}]

Annual calendar: Q1 new year designs + archive restocks. Q2 festival prep (tanks, hats). Q3 back-to-school (hoodies), begin Q4 production. Q4 is 40% of annual revenue — holiday bundles, Black Friday (10-20% off), order-by dates for Christmas delivery.

I want sustainable merch. What are my options? [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise"}]

Sustainable options: Organic cotton blanks (Stanley/Stella, Allmade) at 15-30% premium. Water-based inks. DTG printing for small runs. Recyclable mailers. Gen Z fans specifically seek sustainable merch — it's a selling point, not just a cost.

Should I sell merch on my own site or through third-party platforms? [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise"}]

Own site advantages: Higher margins (no platform cut), you own the customer data/email list, full brand control, bundle flexibility. Platform advantages: Built-in traffic, trust, easier setup. Recommendation: Both. Own site for D2C bundles and exclusives. Bandcamp/Shopify for discovery. Route superfans to your site for best deals.

I want the same hoodie in black, grey, and forest green. Do I need to upload three different files or can you just change the color? My logo is white on all of them. [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise","hasProject":true}]

Great question — whether you need separate files depends on how your design interacts with each garment color:

**Your situation (white logo on black/grey/forest green):**
- If your logo file is white on a transparent background, **one file works for all three colorways** — the print goes on top of whatever garment color you choose
- This is the standard setup for a single-color logo on multiple dark garments

**When you DO need separate files:**
- Your logo has elements that need to change color depending on garment (e.g., outline adjusts to contrast)
- Light garments require a different version (dark artwork instead of white)
- Different print placements per colorway (chest vs. back vs. sleeve)

**File requirements for print production:**
- Format: PNG with transparent background (not white background) OR vector (AI/EPS/SVG)
- Resolution: 300 dpi minimum at print size
- Color mode: RGB for DTG printing; CMYK for screen printing

Send me your white logo file and I'll create mockups across all three colorways from that single asset.

I want to press vinyl. What's involved? [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise"}]

Vinyl pressing: 12-16 week timeline. Vinyl mastering is different from digital (centered bass, 22min/side max). MOQ 100-300 units, $8-15/unit at 300 copies. 140g standard or 180g premium (+$1-2). Color vinyl adds $1-3/unit. Always order test pressings ($50-100). At 300 units cost ~$3K-4.5K, sell at $25-30, break-even at ~150.

I want to press 300 copies of my album on vinyl. What's the real cost breakdown and how should I price it to make money while still being accessible to fans? [Context: {"quantity":300,"product":"vinyl-LP"}]

Vinyl pressing has real economics you need to understand before committing. Here's the full breakdown:

**Production Costs (300 LP run, 2026 pricing):**
- Vinyl pressing (standard 180g black): ~$4,500–5,500 total ($15–18/unit)
- Sleeve design and print (gatefold vs. standard): $800–1,500 ($.2.67–5/unit)
- Insert/lyric sheet: $300–500 ($1–1.67/unit)
- Mastering for vinyl (separate from digital): $200–600
- Setup/test press: $500–800 (one-time)
- **Total investment: ~$6,300–8,800 for 300 units**
- **Cost per unit: ~$21–29**

**Lead Time:** 16–24 weeks is current reality (2026) for vinyl pressing. Plan 6+ months ahead.

**Retail Pricing:**
- $29: Break-even at ~220–250 units. Accessible, leaves ~50 units as profit
- $35: Break-even at ~190 units. $1,800–2,100 net at full sell-through
- $40: Break-even at ~160 units. $3,300+ net at sell-through — strong margin, collector-price territory

**Recommendation for 300 units:** Price at $35. Target: 200 pre-orders before going to press (de-risks the investment).

**Pre-Order Strategy:**
Don't pay for the full pressing out of pocket. Launch a pre-order campaign:
- Target: 150–200 pre-orders = covers ~70% of production cost
- Promise fulfillment in 6 months (realistic with 4-month lead time + buffer)
- Offer incentives for early pre-orders: signed copy, numbered edition

**Distribution Split (if selling through stores):**
- Bandcamp: You keep 85%
- Bandcamp wholesale: ~50% of MSRP if selling to record stores
- Consignment at local record stores: typically 60/40 split (60% to you)

Creating vinyl mockup for pre-order page now.

What I CAN help with:

- Setting up legitimate production and sales channels

I keep making merch but nobody buys it. My t-shirt designs are basically just my artist name in a font on a black tee. What am I doing wrong? [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise","hasProject":true}]

The honest answer: fans don't want to be walking billboards for an artist they have to explain to everyone. The best-selling merch in 2026 is merch people would wear even if they didn't know the artist. Here's how to fix it:

The mindset shift: Think 'streetwear brand' not 'concert souvenir.' Your merch competes with Nike, Stüssy, and every other clothing brand on your fan's Instagram feed. If your design doesn't hold up next to a $40 Urban Outfitters graphic tee, it won't sell.

Design principles for merch that sells: (1) Subtle > obvious. Small logo on the chest, larger graphic on the back. Your artist name should be part of the design, not THE design. (2) Design for the aesthetic, not the ego. What visual world does your music live in? If your music is dark and moody, your merch should reflect that. If it's bright and energetic, use bold colors and patterns. (3) Evergreen > dated. 'Tour 2026' dates your merch immediately. An iconic graphic is wearable forever. (4) Quality matters more than quantity. One great design on a Comfort Colors or Gildan Hammer blank sells better than 5 mediocre designs on cheap blanks. (5) Unisex sizing and cuts. Offer women's fits alongside unisex/men's. You're leaving 40%+ of potential sales on the table without them.

What actually sells in 2026: Graphic tees with artistic imagery (not just text). Embroidered hats (low cost to customize, high perceived value). Crewneck sweatshirts (higher price point = higher profit). Tote bags (eco-conscious, inexpensive, useful). Enamel pins (cheap to produce, collectible, great add-on purchase). Socks (surprisingly popular as merch items).

Design resources if you're not a designer: Fiverr/99designs for affordable graphic design ($30-$200 per design). Canva for simple layouts. Collaborate with visual artists (offer cross-promotion or revenue share). AI image generators for initial concepts (then refine with a human designer).

Test before committing: Use Instagram polls or stories to test 2-3 concepts before manufacturing. Post mockups and see which gets the most engagement. This costs $0 and prevents expensive failures.

I want to do a merch collab with another artist in my scene. We both have similar followings. How do we structure the split and pricing? [Context: {"strategy":"collab-merch","relationship":"peer-artists","followings":"similar"}]

A peer collab merch drop has a clear standard structure. Here's how to set it up fairly:

**The 50/50 Baseline:**
With similar followings, start from a 50/50 revenue share after costs. This is the standard peer collab structure.

**Cost Treatment (Critical — agree upfront):**
Option A: Split production costs 50/50, then split revenue 50/50 net of costs.
Option B: One artist fronts production cost, other pays back their half from first sales, then 50/50 on remaining revenue.

Get this in writing — even a text message — before production starts.

**Who Hosts the Store:**
Decide which artist's Shopify/Bandcamp hosts the product. The hosting artist typically handles customer service, fulfillment, and accounting, which justifies a small administrative fee (5%) if one party is doing significantly more work.

**Product Design:**
Both artists must approve the final design. Establish: (1) who has final design say, (2) what happens if one party doesn't approve, (3) whether either artist's imagery/likeness can be used without approval on future products.

**Accounting Transparency:**
Set up a shared Google Sheet with every transaction logged. Payout: Monthly after the campaign closes, via bank transfer or Venmo/PayPal with receipts.

**Cross-Promotion Requirement:**
Both artists commit to: minimum 3 posts each on launch day, stories, etc. This is the whole point of the collab — the combined audience reach.

**Post-Collab Rights:**
The item should be clearly 'collab edition' — neither artist can sell it independently after the collab period without the other's consent.

Creating both artists' mockup variants now.

Should I build my own online store for merch or just use a platform? I want to sell shirts, hats, vinyl, and maybe some limited drops. [Context: {"currentModule":"merchandise","hasProject":true}]

Having your own store is non-negotiable in 2026 — it's where your highest-margin transactions happen. Here's the platform comparison:

Fourthwall (Recommended for musicians): Monthly fee: $0 (they earn from payment processing at ~3-5%). Built specifically for creators. Handles POD (print-on-demand) AND custom inventory. Integrated memberships/subscriptions. Beautiful templates designed for artist brands. Pros: no monthly cost, creator-focused, built-in fulfillment for POD, excellent mobile experience. Cons: less customizable than Shopify, smaller app ecosystem.

Shopify ($39/month Basic plan): The industry standard for e-commerce. Most customizable, massive app ecosystem. Integrations with Printful, Printify, and every POD service. Pros: infinitely customizable, powerful analytics, integrates with everything, handles high-volume drops. Cons: monthly fee regardless of sales, learning curve, apps add cost.

BigCartel (Free for up to 5 products, $12/month for 50): Designed for independent artists. Dead simple setup. No transaction fees (just payment processing). Pros: free tier, simple, artist-focused. Cons: limited features, basic analytics, fewer integrations.

My recommendation by stage: Just starting (under $500/month merch revenue): Fourthwall or BigCartel Free. Growing ($500-$2,000/month): Fourthwall Pro or Shopify Basic. Scaling ($2,000+/month): Shopify with full app stack.

Limited drop strategy: The most profitable merch model in 2026 is scarcity-driven drops: (1) Announce a limited-edition item 3-5 days before it goes live. (2) Set a specific quantity: 'Only 50 available.' (3) Set a specific window: 'Available for 48 hours.' (4) Use countdown timers on your store page. (5) Promote on all channels with urgency messaging. Limited drops convert 3-5x better than always-available merch.

Essential store features: Clean mobile experience (70%+ of purchases are mobile). Email capture pop-up. Size guides for apparel. Multiple product photos. Shipping calculator. Social proof (customer photos, reviews).
